items.myth: A virtual environment copies the whole Python installation.
items.reality: It mostly links to the base interpreter and creates its own site-packages; it's lightweight, not a full copy.
items.example: `du -sh .venv` on an empty venv shows ~15-25 MB on macOS — mostly symlinks and a tiny pip. Compare to a full Python install (~150 MB).

items.myth: Activating an env changes it permanently for your shell.
items.reality: Activation only affects the current shell session; opening a new terminal starts deactivated.
items.example: Close the terminal, open a new one, run `which python` → back to system Python. You'll need to `source .venv/bin/activate` again (or use direnv / shell hooks).

items.myth: venv and virtualenv are completely different tools.
items.reality: venv is essentially a standard-library subset of virtualenv; they solve the same problem and activation is identical.
items.example: Both produce a directory with `bin/python`, `bin/activate`, and a `site-packages/`. Either's `source bin/activate` works the same way.
